problems.prompt: Solve 3x ‚ąí 4 > 11, then describe its number-line graph (which circle, which way to shade).
problems.solution: Add 4 to both sides: 3x > 15. Divide by +3 (positive ‚Üí no flip): x > 5. The boundary value comes from the matching equation 3x ‚ąí 4 = 11 ‚Üí 3x = 15 ‚Üí x = 5. Strict (>) so an open circle at 5, shade right (toward larger values). Test a point: x = 6 gives 3(6) ‚ąí 4 = 14 > 11. Solution: x > 5.

problems.prompt: Solve ‚ąí4x ‚Č§ 20, then describe its number-line graph.
problems.solution: Divide both sides by ‚ąí4 ‚Üí FLIP the sign (dividing by a negative): x ‚Č• ‚ąí5. The boundary is ‚ąí4x = 20 ‚Üí x = ‚ąí5. Inclusive (‚Č•) so a filled circle at ‚ąí5, shade right. Test a point: x = 0 gives ‚ąí4(0) = 0 ‚Č§ 20, and 0 ‚Č• ‚ąí5 ‚ÄĒ the test agrees, confirming the flip. (Had we forgotten to flip and written x ‚Č§ ‚ąí5, then x = 0 would be wrongly excluded.) Solution: x ‚Č• ‚ąí5.

problems.prompt: Solve 8 ‚ąí x < 3, then describe its number-line graph.
problems.solution: Subtract 8 from both sides: ‚ąíx < ‚ąí5. Divide by ‚ąí1 ‚Üí FLIP: x > 5. The boundary is 8 ‚ąí x = 3 ‚Üí ‚ąíx = ‚ąí5 ‚Üí x = 5. Strict (>) so an open circle at 5, shade right. Test a point: x = 6 gives 8 ‚ąí 6 = 2 < 3, and 6 > 5. (Flip-free route: add x to both sides ‚Üí 8 < 3 + x ‚Üí 5 < x, i.e. x > 5 ‚ÄĒ same answer.) Solution: x > 5.

problems.prompt: Solve 2x + 1 ‚Č• 5x ‚ąí 8 (variable on both sides), then describe its number-line graph.
problems.solution: Collect the variable on one side: subtract 5x from both sides ‚Üí ‚ąí3x + 1 ‚Č• ‚ąí8. Subtract 1 ‚Üí ‚ąí3x ‚Č• ‚ąí9. Divide by ‚ąí3 ‚Üí FLIP: x ‚Č§ 3. The boundary is 2x + 1 = 5x ‚ąí 8 ‚Üí ‚ąí3x = ‚ąí9 ‚Üí x = 3. Inclusive (‚Č§) so a filled circle at 3, shade left. Test a point: x = 0 gives 2(0)+1 = 1 ‚Č• 5(0)‚ąí8 = ‚ąí8, and 0 ‚Č§ 3. Solution: x ‚Č§ 3.

problems.answer: -2 < x < 6 (and; open circles at -2 and 6, shade the segment between)
problems.prompt: Solve the compound inequality ‚ąí3 < x ‚ąí 1 < 5 and describe its graph (segment-between for and, two rays for or).
problems.solution: This is a three-part 'and'. Do the same move to all three parts: add 1 to each ‚Üí ‚ąí3 + 1 < x < 5 + 1, i.e. ‚ąí2 < x < 6. Both ends strict, so open circles at ‚ąí2 and 6, shade the segment between (an 'and' = the overlap, one piece). Test a point inside: x = 0 gives ‚ąí3 < 0 ‚ąí 1 = ‚ąí1 < 5. Solution: ‚ąí2 < x < 6.

problems.answer: 0 <= x <= 4 (and; filled circles at 0 and 4, shade the segment between)
problems.prompt: Solve 1 ‚Č§ 3x + 1 ‚Č§ 13 and describe its graph.
problems.solution: Three-part 'and'. Subtract 1 from all three parts: 0 ‚Č§ 3x ‚Č§ 12. Divide all three by +3 (positive ‚Üí no flip): 0 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 4. Both ends inclusive, so filled circles at 0 and 4, shade the segment between. Test a point: x = 2 gives 1 ‚Č§ 3(2)+1 = 7 ‚Č§ 13. Solution: 0 ‚Č§ x ‚Č§ 4.

problems.answer: x <= -2 or x > 3 (or; filled circle at -2 shading left, open circle at 3 shading right; two rays)
problems.prompt: Solve 3x ‚Č§ ‚ąí6 or x ‚ąí 2 > 1 and describe its graph.
problems.solution: This is an 'or' ‚ÄĒ solve each part separately, then union. Left: 3x ‚Č§ ‚ąí6 ‚Üí divide by +3 ‚Üí x ‚Č§ ‚ąí2. Right: x ‚ąí 2 > 1 ‚Üí add 2 ‚Üí x > 3. The two rays don't touch, so the solution is everything in either: x ‚Č§ ‚ąí2 (filled circle at ‚ąí2, shade left) or x > 3 (open circle at 3, shade right). Test: x = ‚ąí5 satisfies the left part (3(‚ąí5) = ‚ąí15 ‚Č§ ‚ąí6); x = 10 satisfies the right (10 ‚ąí 2 = 8 > 1). Solution: x ‚Č§ ‚ąí2 or x > 3.

problems.answer: -1 <= x < 4 (three-part divide by -2, FLIP both signs, rewrite increasing; filled circle at -1, open circle at 4, shade between)
problems.prompt: Solve ‚ąí8 < ‚ąí2x ‚Č§ 2 and describe its graph. (Light stretch: dividing a three-part inequality by a negative flips BOTH signs.)
problems.solution: Divide all three parts by ‚ąí2 ‚Üí FLIP both signs: ‚ąí8/(‚ąí2) > x ‚Č• 2/(‚ąí2), i.e. 4 > x ‚Č• ‚ąí1. Rewrite left-to-right in increasing order (smaller number on the left): reading 4 > x ‚Č• ‚ąí1 backwards gives ‚ąí1 ‚Č§ x < 4. Only the positions move, so the strict end stays strict (4 keeps its open mark) and the inclusive end stays inclusive (‚ąí1 keeps its filled mark). Graph: filled circle at ‚ąí1, open circle at 4, shade the segment between. Test inside: x = 0 gives ‚ąí8 < ‚ąí2(0) = 0 ‚Č§ 2. Endpoint x = ‚ąí1: ‚ąí2(‚ąí1) = 2, and ‚ąí8 < 2 ‚Č§ 2 (‚ąí1 is in); endpoint x = 4: ‚ąí2(4) = ‚ąí8, and ‚ąí8 < ‚ąí8 is false (4 is out). Solution: ‚ąí1 ‚Č§ x < 4.

problems.prompt: Solve |x| = 9 (give both solutions) and check them.
problems.solution: Read |x| as distance from 0: 'which numbers are 9 units from 0?' Both ways ‚Üí x = 9 or x = ‚ąí9. Check: |9| = 9 and |‚ąí9| = 9. Solution: x = 9 or x = ‚ąí9.

problems.prompt: Solve 4|x| = 20 (isolate the absolute value first), give both solutions.
problems.solution: Isolate |x| first: divide both sides by 4 (positive ‚Üí no flip) ‚Üí |x| = 5. Now read it as distance 5 from 0, both ways ‚Üí x = 5 or x = ‚ąí5. Check: 4|5| = 20 and 4|‚ąí5| = 20. Solution: x = 5 or x = ‚ąí5.

problems.answer: -7 < x < 7 (within; open circles at -7 and 7, shade the segment between -- an 'and')
problems.prompt: Solve |x| < 7 and describe its graph.
problems.solution: Read it as distance from 0: 'which numbers are LESS than 7 units from 0?' Within 7 of 0 in both directions ‚Üí ‚ąí7 < x < 7. 'Within / less-than' becomes an AND (one segment). Both ends strict ‚Üí open circles at ‚ąí7 and 7, shade the segment between. Test a point inside: x = 0 gives |0| = 0 < 7; x = 7 gives |7| = 7 < 7 is false (excluded, as the open circle shows). Solution: ‚ąí7 < x < 7.

problems.answer: x <= -5 or x >= 5 (isolate: divide by 2 -> |x|>=5; 'outside' is an 'or'; filled circles at -5 and 5, two rays)
problems.prompt: Solve 2|x| ‚Č• 10 (isolate first, then read it) and describe its graph. (Light stretch: combine isolating with the 'outside ‚Üí or' reading.)
problems.solution: Isolate |x| first: divide both sides by 2 (positive ‚Üí no flip) ‚Üí |x| ‚Č• 5. Read it as distance from 0: 'which numbers are AT LEAST 5 units from 0?' That's everything 5 or more from 0 in either direction ‚Üí x ‚Č§ ‚ąí5 or x ‚Č• 5. 'Outside / greater-than' becomes an OR (two rays). Both ends inclusive ‚Üí filled circles at ‚ąí5 and 5, shade outward. Test: x = ‚ąí6 gives 2|‚ąí6| = 12 ‚Č• 10; x = 0 gives 2|0| = 0 ‚Č• 10 is false (correctly outside the solution). Solution: x ‚Č§ ‚ąí5 or x ‚Č• 5.

problems.prompt: Graph y > 4x + 2: name the boundary line, say dashed or solid, do the origin test out loud, and which side to shade. (The eval finds the boundary point at x = ‚ąí1.)
problems.solution: Boundary: replace > with = ‚Üí y = 4x + 2. It is dashed because the inequality is strict (>), so the line itself is not included. Two points to plot: (0, 2) and, computing at x = ‚ąí1, y = 4(‚ąí1) + 2 = ‚ąí2 ‚Üí (‚ąí1, ‚ąí2). Test the origin (0,0): is 0 > 4(0) + 2 = 2? False ‚Üí shade the side NOT containing the origin (above the dashed line). Solution: dashed boundary y = 4x + 2; shade the half-plane above it.

problems.prompt: Graph y ‚Č§ ‚ąí3x + 5: name the boundary line, dashed or solid, do the origin test, and which side to shade. (The eval finds the boundary point at x = 2.)
problems.solution: Boundary: y = ‚ąí3x + 5. It is solid because the inequality is inclusive (‚Č§), so the line is included. Two points: (0, 5) and, at x = 2, y = ‚ąí3(2) + 5 = ‚ąí1 ‚Üí (2, ‚ąí1). Test the origin (0,0): is 0 ‚Č§ ‚ąí3(0) + 5 = 5? True ‚Üí shade the side CONTAINING the origin (below the solid line). Solution: solid boundary y = ‚ąí3x + 5; shade the half-plane below it (and the line itself).

problems.prompt: Graph y < ¬ľx ‚ąí 3 (fractional slope): name the boundary, dashed or solid, do the origin test, and which side to shade. (The eval finds the boundary point at x = 8.)
problems.solution: Boundary: y = ¬ľx ‚ąí 3. It is dashed (strict <). Pick x-values that clear the fraction: at x = 0, y = ‚ąí3 ‚Üí (0, ‚ąí3); at x = 8, y = ¬ľ(8) ‚ąí 3 = 2 ‚ąí 3 = ‚ąí1 ‚Üí (8, ‚ąí1). Test the origin (0,0): is 0 < ¬ľ(0) ‚ąí 3 = ‚ąí3? False ‚Üí shade the OTHER side (below the dashed line). Solution: dashed boundary y = ¬ľx ‚ąí 3; shade the half-plane below it.

problems.answer: System y > x - 2 (dashed) and y <= -x + 4 (solid); solution is the overlap wedge containing the origin -- e.g. (1,1) satisfies both
problems.prompt: Graph the system y > x ‚ąí 2 and y ‚Č§ ‚ąíx + 4. Describe the solution region (which boundaries, dashed or solid, and where the overlap sits). (Light stretch: a system is the overlap, not the union.)
problems.solution: Boundary 1: y = x ‚ąí 2, dashed (strict >). Test (0,0): is 0 > 0 ‚ąí 2 = ‚ąí2? True ‚Üí shade the side with the origin (above this line). Boundary 2: y = ‚ąíx + 4, solid (inclusive ‚Č§). Test (0,0): is 0 ‚Č§ ‚ąí0 + 4 = 4? True ‚Üí shade the side with the origin (below this line). The solution is the OVERLAP of the two shaded regions ‚ÄĒ a wedge containing the origin, bounded below by the dashed line y = x ‚ąí 2 and above by the solid line y = ‚ąíx + 4. Confirm with a point inside, e.g. (1,1): 1 > 1 ‚ąí 2 = ‚ąí1 and 1 ‚Č§ ‚ąí1 + 4 = 3. Solution: the overlapping wedge (the dashed lower edge excluded, the solid upper edge included).

problems.prompt: Solve ‚ąí2x + 3 > 11 and describe its number-line graph. (Mixes 8.1 solving with the negative-coefficient flip.)
problems.solution: Subtract 3 from both sides: ‚ąí2x > 8. Divide by ‚ąí2 ‚Üí FLIP: x < ‚ąí4. The boundary is ‚ąí2x + 3 = 11 ‚Üí ‚ąí2x = 8 ‚Üí x = ‚ąí4. Strict (<) so an open circle at ‚ąí4, shade left. Test a point: x = ‚ąí5 gives ‚ąí2(‚ąí5) + 3 = 13 > 11, and ‚ąí5 < ‚ąí4 ‚ÄĒ the test confirms the flip. Solution: x < ‚ąí4.

problems.prompt: Solve |x| = 6 (give both solutions) and check. (8.3 absolute value as distance from 0.)
problems.solution: Distance from 0: which numbers are 6 units from 0? Both ways ‚Üí x = 6 or x = ‚ąí6. Check: |6| = 6 and |‚ąí6| = 6. Solution: x = 6 or x = ‚ąí6.

problems.answer: x < -2 or x > 5 (or; open circle at -2 shading left, open circle at 5 shading right; two rays)
problems.prompt: Solve x + 4 < 2 or 2x > 10 and describe its graph. (8.2 compound 'or'.)
problems.solution: An 'or' ‚ÄĒ solve each part, then union. Left: x + 4 < 2 ‚Üí subtract 4 ‚Üí x < ‚ąí2. Right: 2x > 10 ‚Üí divide by +2 ‚Üí x > 5. The pieces don't touch, so the solution is everything in either: x < ‚ąí2 (open circle at ‚ąí2, shade left) or x > 5 (open circle at 5, shade right). Test: x = ‚ąí3 makes the left true (‚ąí3 + 4 = 1 < 2); x = 6 makes the right true (2(6) = 12 > 10). Solution: x < ‚ąí2 or x > 5.

problems.prompt: Graph y ‚Č• 2x ‚ąí 5: name the boundary, dashed or solid, do the origin test, and which side to shade. (8.4 ‚ÄĒ the eval finds the boundary point at x = 3.)
problems.solution: Boundary: y = 2x ‚ąí 5, solid because the inequality is inclusive (‚Č•). Two points: (0, ‚ąí5) and, at x = 3, y = 2(3) ‚ąí 5 = 1 ‚Üí (3, 1). Test the origin (0,0): is 0 ‚Č• 2(0) ‚ąí 5 = ‚ąí5? True ‚Üí shade the side CONTAINING the origin (above the solid line). Solution: solid boundary y = 2x ‚ąí 5; shade the half-plane above it (line included).

problems.answer: -7 < x < 7 (isolate: divide by 2 -> |x|<7; 'within' is an 'and'; open circles at -7 and 7, shade between)
problems.prompt: Solve 2|x| < 14 (isolate first, then read it) and describe its graph. (Light stretch interleaving 8.3 absolute value with 8.2 compound 'and'.)
problems.solution: Isolate the absolute value first: divide both sides by 2 (positive ‚Üí no flip) ‚Üí |x| < 7. Read it as distance from 0: which numbers are LESS than 7 from 0? Within 7 in both directions ‚Üí ‚ąí7 < x < 7. 'Within / less-than' becomes an AND (one segment). Both ends strict ‚Üí open circles at ‚ąí7 and 7, shade the segment between. Test inside: x = 0 gives 2|0| = 0 < 14; x = 7 gives 2|7| = 14 < 14 is false (correctly excluded). Solution: ‚ąí7 < x < 7.
